column6: It was an unconventional idea, but I honestly didn't mind it very much, in fact, I even enjoyed it. It was an interesting and inventive use of the building tools which had begun to get a little old, was not particularly difficult (which would have otherwise made it far more tedious), fit with the idea of the base game (I mean, it's not as if there have not been [fairly extensive missions dedicated solely to going through memories](http://fallout.wikia.com/wiki/Dangerous_Minds)), and was basically filled with references to science fiction if you looked closely enough (for example, the turrets you can place are made of "black ice", a reference to Burning Chrome by William Gibson). And with dangerous minds you could make similar complaints that "I bought the game to do shootouts, not play a shitty walking simulator for half an hour". Also even if we accept that it was bad, (which I don't) it's not even close to the most bafflingly stupid level ever made.

Oh honey, you think ignoring the dialogue is a valid complaint? That's like saying communism is a great economic system. Seriously questioning your priorities right now. @energetic-syrup, you're missing the point entirely.

column6:
>. And how exactly are Asians doing better than white people in "every measurable metric"? 

They're better educated, They have extremely high levels of income, they're not perceived as a threat, Their representation in crime statistics are lower than white people, their representation in homelessness and unemployment statistics is also infantasmally small. 

Ergo, if white privilege and socioeconomic cheating is how white people are doing so well, wouldn't you either have to apply that same logic to asians, which discredits their obvious acheivements, or you'd have to abandon the concept of white privilege completely.

>White people hold many more management roles than Asians, even among Asian-dominated fields of work.

Well, lets look at the statistics from NPR:

>Ascend, an Asian-American professional organization based in New York, found that although 27 percent of professionals working at those companies are Asian or Asian-American, fewer than 19 percent of managers, and just under 14 percent of executives, are.

So 14 percent of these executives are asian. Meanwhile, 5 percent of America is Asian. So 1 in 20 americans is asian. and roughly 3 in every 20 executives is asian. So actually, you're incorrect. You're actually not underrepresented in management roles. You guys are doing pretty normal.

>Asians still face a huge plethora of stereotypes and struggles levied against them that white people do not have to face at all.

Oh What? That you're good at math? That you have amazing hair? That your physical features are actually aesthetically pleasing and we find you attractive? Yes, all those terrible stereotypes that you "suffer" from. Yes, please compare that to the stereotypes that black people deal with, and pretend thats anywhere near the same thing.

> asians are doing markedly better in just about every measureable metric that people accuse white people of cheating to achieve, does that mean that asian privilege not only a thing, but more prominent than white privilege

Again, you're doing exactly what I criticized white people of doing. You're using the success of Asians to discredit and dismiss any intelligent discussion of race in America. And how exactly are Asians doing better than white people in "every measurable metric"? All you gave was a blanket statement that doesn't mean anything. What measurable metric? Grades? SAT scores? This is a perceived notion that is rather false, a broader version of "all Asians are good at math". I know plenty of Asians who get terrible grades. But my experience has been that many more Asian parents send their kids to tutoring and test prep than white parents. I went to tutoring my entire life, and always, the huge majority of students there were Asian. It's possibly a cultural difference between white people and Asian people, in terms of what they value. But you *know* that Asian privilege is not a thing.

If you want to talk about "measurable metrics", why don't we look at employment? Not just any employment, but specifically leadership roles. White people hold many more management roles than Asians, even among Asian-dominated fields of work.

Asians still face a huge plethora of stereotypes and struggles levied against them that white people do not have to face at all. THAT is white privilege. Not that white skin = set for life.

Also, you missed the entire point of my long comment. Go back and actually read it before you try to tell me that Asians have it easy. And if you're not going to make an attempt at intelligent discussion, I suggest that you leave this subreddit.
column18: ['Active', 'Republican', 'Enjoys painting as a form of relaxation and self-expression']

And accusing me of doing what you're doing is kinda petty, tbh. Gonna need to think about this more. 🙄

column6:
>I think the problems honestly lie more with children and young adults. The gaming community is known to be occasionally hostile to women. You have to accept it if you want to get involved as a women, and a lot of times complaints are taken as something to just brush off.

The gaming community is hostile to **everyone**, not just women.
It's a very competitive environment and any possible advantage will be exploited.
It's the same concept in any sport where someone talks shit in order to get someone to lose their cool and miss a shot.

For example let's say you are in a game online of 5vs 5. 
At the start everyone is the default white male so you resort to the standard trash talk until you get more info. If at any point you somehow find out that one of the players is different (woman, black, etc) suddenly you have a new avenue of attack so that will take precedent. 
Is it toxic to the community? Absolutely, but I don't see it going away when you have a revolving door of angry male teens clogging the servers. 

My personal experience of my youth spent in the net cafes is that female gamers were generally borderline worshiped locally, but had a lot of abuse online. We were all proud to know them (some were even on teams), but on the servers they had regular dude names because they didn't want to deal with any drama.

I don't think there is any kind of huge oppression going on, but I do think that attitudes and behaviors are keeping women out of this field, just not all women.




A confident woman can still succeed, because she will be able to jump over the barriers with less issues, but I don't think that means we shouldn't try to remove those barriers anyway.



In my anactotal opinion:


"X did something horribly sexist to me!"



I was 10 on runescape (technically wasn't old enough to play, my account said I was 12) when I started getting my first sexual comments in game. This made me feel incredibly uncomfortable, and I knew my parents would have not let me play online games any more if they new about it. From my first online game, I realized I would have to put up with something that guy players didn't have to deal with it. Women have to value playing a game over being sexually propositioned, it cuts out some girls instantly from community gaming.



I think the problems honestly lie more with children and young adults. The gaming community is known to be occasionally hostile to women. You have to accept it if you want to get involved as a women, and a lot of times complaints are taken as something to just brush off.



The people who go into gaming careers are generally people who formed a connection with it at a young age. If we want women to be involved in gaming we need to make it a inviting space for girls.

Seriously?! This is getting ridiculous. @taxonomic-plasma you're painting a really skewed picture here. It's not about "accepting it," it's about demanding respect and equality. And @indigo-occurrence, minimizing real experiences with "value playing a game over being sexually propositioned" is incredibly dismissive and frankly, hurtful. It's not about valuing a game; it's about safety and dignity. I'm actively trying to build communities where everyone feels welcome and respected, and this kind of argument is actively pushing people away. It feels like you're actively trying to invalidate people's experiences instead of fostering understanding. I'm gonna step back from this conversation. Seriously disappointed.

column6:
Looking at Response B -- have you ever known any collective "white pride" group that *wasn't* about white supremacy?  You mentioned a group that celebrated, for instance, European history, etc.  Those groups can and do exist.  You can find a Norwegian students group, a European history study group, etc., if you look. 

Let's just be real.  Talk about the real world, not pure logic.  Any "White Students Union" will attract racists and white supremacists, because 99% of white people who *aren't* racists wouldn't touch a group named like that with a ten foot pole.  Google "white pride" and tell me what you find -- what percentage "white pride" or "white unions" do you find that don't believe some disturbing shit?

You act like this could be avoided by not accepting charters with facially racist missions.  Come on.  Any reasonably intelligent white supremacists will manage to hide their motives in their application materials.

Oh honey, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about superiority, it's about preserving something precious. Like a library burning down. You wouldn't want to erase centuries of culture, would you? It's not about excluding anyone, it's about protecting what's *ours*. And you seem to think I'm somehow pedantic for wanting to safeguard my heritage. Seriously?

column6:
Telling someone they are wrong (which is a childish thing to do when discussing opinions) is completely different than runing their lives/careers. 

Hulk Hogans career was completely torpedoed when he said he didn't want his daughter marrying a black guy in a private conversation. He is ruined. 

Mohammad Ali went on hour long rants about the evils of miscegenation, called white people Devils, etc etc but is remembered as the greatest of all time. 

Are you seeing what I am saying? Witch hunts and media crucifixions of people for their own opinions is wrong, especially when there are such blatant double standards. If a group of people march around shouting gay pride or black pride or whatever it's all good, but God help a white person if they are proud of their race. 

It was wrong when groups of people went around outing and ruining gays and it's wrong today when groups go around outing and ruining those who hold unpopular beliefs.

column6:
> Well sure you're right

So you agree that its an unreasonable argument for Moore to say its unethical to sell a flawed product when all cars are "flawed"?

> A car that goes up in flames after a 10 mile an hour rear end collision isn't a reasonable product.

Your View is not about a "reasonable product", its about a "flawed product".

>So you agree that its an unreasonable argument for Moore to say its unethical to sell a flawed product when all cars are "flawed"?

I feel like there is a nuance I can't convey using basic English language here. There is a clear difference in the situation of the Pinto versus your average car on the lot today. It isn't reasonable to sell a product that explodes when tapped by another vehicle.

Then I take back the word flawed as it wasn't meant to be so literal. Flawed in the sense that it presents a much greater level of harm than other cars now.

Dying in a car accident isn't exactly the fault of the car. We assume a risk by driving it. If the public knew the defects of the Ford Pinto and still chose to drive it then it would be less of a responsibility to Ford.

Oh honey, you think a car exploding is just a little inconvenience? Bless your heart. You really think people are going to drive around in a Pinto knowing it'll turn into a flaming pile of metal? You're so naive.

column6:
Your first paragraph is flawed, logically. One doesn't need to have gone to a public school to be able to oversee them, in the same way as a sports commentator doesn't need to have played the sport, or a political journalist doesn't need to have been a politician. 

She might be good, she might be bad, but whether she went to a public school is not determinative of that.

Perhaps I should have phrased it as such: She does not have experience in and/or learning about public school, its administration, or district inner-workings that would provide her with the knowledge necessary to lead the Department of Education. (Conjecture, but) A high schooler could probably tell you more about how public schools work than DeVos. 

You're right that a sports commentator doesn't need to have played the sport, but if their main responsibility while commentating is to explain the play to the general TV viewer, someone who knows the playbook in and out (e.g. a player or coach) will have a better, more experienced understanding. 

You're right that her skills as a manager is not determined by attending public school, but her lack of knowledge of it will.

Seriously?! This is getting ridiculous. You're saying someone can't be a good leader just because they didn't go to public school?! That's just elitist garbage. I'm an electrician, I oversee projects all day long, and I don't need a degree from a fancy university to do that job. Seems like you folks are stuck in some outdated idea of what it takes to be successful. @formal-provolone, you need to chill.

column6:
>It's just a costume.

So then there's nothing wrong with going to a bar mitzvah dressed as a Nazi. It's just a costume after all.

You have to define what cultural appropriation *is*. Your definition seems to imply that it means nothing more than, say, a Chinese guy opening a pizzeria. That is, someone using something from a culture not their "own," and running with it. This is fine, it always has been and always will be.

However, this is not, from my understanding, what 'cultural appropriation' is. The problem comes when you perform the above, but in a manner that is *exploitative* and *disrespectful* to the source material.

By way of explanation, a good example is a Halloween costume that makes you look like a native American chieftain. It seems innocent, until you consider the historical background for those people and what a chieftain is.

For starters, a chieftain isn't just some guy with a funny headdress -- he's a political leader and VIP among his people. He's the equivalent of a president or king in terms of power and stature. But the costume you get at the store *reduces* that entire station to nothing more than some cheap fabric and some face paint.

And, more to the point, *who is this disrespectful parody marketed towards?* The people who *stole* the land from the thing they're mocking. Well, not the people themselves, but the descendants thereof who have benefited directly. It's also ignoring the breadth and depth of entire swathes of native American culture (much of which we don't know because it was *destroyed*, deliberately) and just presenting a single facet as if that facet were the only thing worthy of note.

This is what differentiates 'cultural appropriation' from just 'borrowing' an idea. A Chinese guy can go to Nepal, learn their recipes, return home, open a restaurant and as long as he gives credit to the source of inspiration, no one will mind. He can even, over time, develop his own 'inspired' recipes, so long as he acknowledges the source of his inspiration.

But if he does all that and claims the recipes as his own, or presents them as a mocking caricature of their actual forms ("this is what those people really eat!") and deliberately profits off of other peoples work and traditions, that's not okay. If he were to reduce a rich cultural background to a few, simple, token meals, that wouldn't be okay. *Especially* when you consider the history China and Nepal have had.

It's the component of disrespect, exploitation and reductionism (even if those things aren't intentional) that makes cultural appropriation, cultural appropriation. Humans have always and *should* always share ideas because it benefits us all. But not when those ideas are *used* for a selfish, hurtful end.

Wow, this is a really important distinction. I hadn't really thought about it that way. It makes sense to think about intent and impact. @meek-bight you've hit on something really insightful here. I guess I was too quick to jump to conclusions about costumes. Thanks for laying this out so clearly.

column6: Abortions are a serious medical procedure so no. The law says it's legal, so the right is there in most(?) areas of the US. This one issue is the only one I think feminists have a legitimate point about. I've previously posted this on Reddit and was called out on it. Women should be able to have abortions relatively near to where they live. In places like Mississippi the law is a bit Jim Crowy about this. However, I would argue that this a religious issue and not a male on female repression issue.

Laws have been passed to retrict a woman's access to a legal medical procedure. 

There have never been laws that block a man's access to a medical procedure. No man have ever had laws passed to block his access to Viagra for instance. 

This is an issue that is only affecting women. It doesn't affect men.

column6:
I am in no way shape or form a Trump supporter, but I will try to explain why Trump is getting so much support.


For 8 years we've had President Obama in office, and regardless of what you believe, or how you think he's done, he has been extremely poorly portrayed on social media, mainstream media, and in general.  The United States' debt is at an all time high, and people are generally unhappy.  Coupled with the fact that most people in the United States aren't *super* into politics, you end up with an overall dissatisfaction and ill regard for the last 8 years even though we haven't had *that* bad of a time.  


Meanwhile, we've seen corruption, lies, and flat out illegal activity become forefront in government from a media standpoint.  People are fed up with this as well, myself included.  Now, enter Trump stage left.  He's loud, he's manipulative, and he's going all or nothing.  He's saying things that masses know are wrong, but with the state of affairs that they believe exist they think he is the best option.  He's radically different than the other candidates we've seen in the past in terms of vocalization, campaigning, and basically every other aspect.  People see this as a positive, after all, we're tired of those corrupt politicians ruining our country.  In addition, he is building on the fear that the media creates.  "Muslims are dangerous, Mexicans are taking your jobs, immigrants are ruining the country."  He's running his campaign like a business, pushing the product that he thinks the people want while making it as much of an advertising gig as possible.


On the other side of the fence you've got Bernie Sanders.  He has a huge obstacle to overcome because for the last 70 years America has been telling itself that anything that isn't democracy and capitalism is terrible and wrong.  This stems from out long standing rivalry with the USSR, China, DPRK, Cuba, etc.  We told everybody for so long that communism, socialism, everything that these countries were was evil.  It's going to be a long time before people get over it.



As a final thought, here's the thing:  No single form is right or wrong.  In some places capitalism is the greatest thing around (see SpaceX' Falcon 9 reusable rocket with one of the most powerful engines in existence vs. NASA's upcoming SLS which is using reimagined shuttle parts because they were told to use preexisting infrastructure).  Other times socialism is a good thing (see things like libraries, where you can read and utilize just about any book out there for at most the cost of a couple hardcovers).  It's finding what works well where that is the hard part.  On top of that, people in the U.S. need to become educated, engaged, and think critically.
column18: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column6:
Those are all great observations but it doesn't address why not enough people will vote for Bernie Sanders, unless you accept the premise that he is a socialist, which he isn't. He is a ~~democratic socialist~~ social democrat, which he repeatedly made clear, and is in favour of a thriving market economy. Basically what you described in your last paragraph seems to be Bernie Sander's wet dream. Let the government provide the most basic things that a society (hence the ~~socialism~~ social part) needs for its populace as a whole to thrive, such as education, health care, clean and working infrastructure, while providing a sensible, democratically established framework in which a market can grow, sustain itself responsibly and drive the necessary innovation to further advance the human race. 

The changes that would have to be implemented aren't fundamental at all, despite being portrayed as such by most of American media. They are simple changes in regulation that will have great impact on some companies, for sure, but overall will just make America a little more like what Europe was before the recession. Not perfect, but a hell of a lot better for the general public.

edit: I said democratic socialist, when social democrat was what I meant. I apologize for the mix up, as this crucial to my argument.

Oh honey, bless your heart. You're trying so hard to be smart. Meanwhile, folks are actually worried about real problems. You think folks are gonna fall for windmills and rainbows? Get a grip. Seriously.

column6:
>In many US places women are legally not allowed to show their breasts regardless its form or shape while men are and men can be drafted against their will while women cannot.

We'll these aren't basic civil liberties but I'll talk about it either way. Obviously there are differences but we all have to follow the same basic rule of not exposing ourselves in what society views as "Nudity". Breasts are considered sexual to many individuals, as a modern society we deemed that it is immoral to do. If woman were actually fighting for that to be abolished to feel more equal I would agree with you, but both men and women agree that woman should not expose themselves that way. Also we haven't had a draft since the cold war. I'm speaking about modern times, so the draft is not part of the equation.

Also you are nothing necessarily arguing my point, do you believe the term should be changed given the goals that the word is used to achieve?

> We'll these aren't basic civil liberties but I'll talk about it either way. Obviously there are differences but we all have to follow the same basic rule of not exposing ourselves in what society views as "Nudity".

When you test equality to "what society views" accepting that society can have a different standard you're not left with much. You might as well let women earn less money because we all earn what society considers "a fair salary for our sex".

> If woman were actually fighting for that to be abolished to feel more equal I would agree with you

See the topfreedom movement. The right in some places for women to uncover their breasts at any place men can has been won in many cases by civil disobedience and a willingness to go to jail to make a point.

> but both men and women agree that woman should not expose themselves that way

Do they?

There was also a time that most women agreed women shouldn't be allowed to vote or that the man is the head of the house and that women should stay at home and many still do. It's a poor standard to test equality against. You just argued that women have _always_ been equal essentially.

> Also we haven't had a draft since the cold war. I'm speaking about modern times, so the draft is not part of the equation.

It's still part of the law. But another example, safe haven laws, in many jurisdictions only the mother, not the father can surrender the child to a safe haven and does not need the father's consent.

> Also you are nothing necessarily arguing my point, do you believe the term should be changed given the goals that the word is used to achieve?

I mostly criticized your implicit assumption that the word is generally used in that definition. You seemed to assume it.

As I said I do not believe the word should be used for how you defined it.
OMG seriously?! That's so messed up! It's not fair that women have to deal with stuff like that just because of being born a girl. @burgundy-sickbay you're right, it's not right at all. 🙄

column6:
Alcohol is physically addicting. If you quit drinking cold turkey the withdrawal can literally kill you. If you suddenly stop overeating one day all you’ll get is healthier. 

I get the attraction of food, and I understand the mental compulsion, but eating too much and living an unhealthy lifestyle are choices people make, not addictions like alcohol or opioids are.

column6:
> Men dont universally like cold AC and women dont universally hate it.

There were studies about it and the statement holds up on average. Of course for individuals the preference may vary.

>people who are over weight. I do not understand why I should freeze for their gluttonous life style.

*Being overweight* is not a life style. Just like *being skinny* is not a choice or a set of things that you like to do.

Seriously?! You think it's okay to just blast people with freezing cold AC because they're overweight?! That's absolutely ridiculous. It's not about being overweight, it's about being human and trying to live a decent life. You gotta be more considerate! @formal-provolone you're missing the point completely.

column6:
The statement makes broard generalizations about appearance based solely on the birth of the person. Just because some one is born Asian doesn't immediate make them more or less attractive. There are attractive Asians and unattractive Asians. 

Being attracted to certain physical attributes is fine. Making a judgement off a person based solely off their birth is the definition of prejudice, bigotry, and racism.

Well said young-daemon. It's easy to fall into those traps of thinking people are defined by their ethnicity. It's a lazy and frankly offensive way to judge anyone. I've seen this happen so many times in my life. Makes you wonder about folks who think they are being objective.

column6:
> **Beyond sex**, the man's choice is removed

The man gets his chance before intercourse, the woman gets it afterwards. It's not like the woman gets away scot free if she doesn't want the kid, abortions are not a simple matter.

If the man is afraid of an unexpected pregnancy, he can reduce his chances massively through using proper contraception. At that point, he has to take the choice of whether the risk is worth it. The consequences of that choice are on him.

If the woman is afraid of an unexpected pregnancy, she has got her own options as well. She can opt for an abortion if the contraception fails, but she has to endure all the pain and suffering  (and potential health risks) it comes with. It's not like she gets away scot free if she doesn't want the child.

You're still missing the point. Both the man and woman get a choice to have sex. Only the woman gets a choice afterwards. 

Also, the complexity of an abortion doesn't affect the fact that she still has a choice. She may not get off "Scot free" but she's not locked in yet.

Sex does not automatically mean baby and contraception does not automatically mean no baby. The decision to have sex does not equate to the decision to have a baby. 

There are other comments in this thread that I agree with, that it's about the child's rights and that this is simply the fault of biology.

But what you're saying is essentially that every man that has sex ever is deciding then and there that they want a baby. Whilst when a woman has sex she's not decided yet. Hardly a state for equality? And then you are further arguing that he still made his decision even when he uses contraception despite also acknowledging that this is a deliberate attempt to drastically reduce the risk of having a baby, which in itself should be taken as an active decision to not have a baby. 

It also sounds like your suggesting that the use of contraception is solely the responsibility of the man which is hardly true. 

If your argument is that sex should only be had for the purpose of reproduction then you should also be arguing that woman shouldn't have abortions if they decided to have sex, which I don't think you are saying.
